quiz.title: Geometry: End-of-Year Review — Proofs and Congruence
quiz.subtitle: Unit 12 Quiz 7: Comprehensive Review of Units 2, 4, and 5 (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 12.7 End-of-Year Review: Proofs and Congruence

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$, $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, $\angle A \cong \angle D$, and $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{DF}$. Which congruence postulate proves $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$?
questions.explanation: We have two sides ($\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$ and $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{DF}$) and the included angle ($\angle A \cong \angle D$) between them. This matches the SAS (Side-Angle-Side) postulate. The angle must be between the two sides for SAS to apply.

questions.question: In a two-column proof, you have proven $\triangle PQR \cong \triangle STU$. To conclude that $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{ST}$, which reason should be cited?
questions.explanation: CPCTC is used after proving two triangles congruent. It allows you to conclude that any pair of corresponding parts (sides or angles) are congruent. It is not a method to prove triangles congruent — it is a consequence of congruence.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$, $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$. Which statement must be true?
questions.explanation: If two sides of a triangle are congruent, the triangle is isosceles. The Isosceles Triangle Theorem states that the angles opposite those congruent sides are also congruent. Since $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, the angles opposite them — $\angle C$ and $\angle B$ — are congruent.

questions.question: In a proof, $\overline{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$. What must be true?
questions.explanation: An angle bisector divides an angle into two congruent angles. If $\overline{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$, then $\angle ABD \cong \angle DBC$. Each of these two angles is exactly half of $\angle ABC$.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. If $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are alternate interior angles, which statement is true?
questions.explanation: The Alternate Interior Angles Theorem states that when two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, alternate interior angles are congruent. This is one of the fundamental angle relationship theorems used extensively in triangle proofs.

questions.question: In right $\triangle ABC$ and right $\triangle DEF$, the right angles are at $C$ and $F$. If $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$ and $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$, which theorem proves the triangles congruent?
questions.explanation: HL applies specifically to right triangles. The hypotenuse ($\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$) and one leg ($\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$) are congruent. This is sufficient to prove the right triangles congruent. HL is a shortcut that avoids having to prove the third sides congruent.

questions.question: In $\triangle JKL$, $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{JK}$. What does this imply?
questions.explanation: By definition, a midpoint divides a segment into two congruent segments. If $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{JK}$, then $\overline{JM} \cong \overline{MK}$. This is often used in proofs to establish a pair of congruent sides.

questions.question: Which pair of corresponding parts is NOT sufficient by itself to prove two triangles congruent?
questions.explanation: AAA proves similarity, not congruence. Two triangles can have the same angle measures but be different sizes (similar triangles). All four valid congruence postulates/theorems — SSS, SAS, ASA, and AAS (and HL for right triangles) — require at least one pair of congruent sides.

questions.question: In the figure, $\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD} \parallel \overline{BC}$. Which must be true?
options: All four angles are right angles
questions.explanation: When both pairs of opposite sides are parallel, the figure is a parallelogram. In a parallelogram, opposite angles are congruent. The figure could be a rectangle, rhombus, or square, but the given information only guarantees a parallelogram — so only opposite angles must be congruent.

questions.question: In a two-column proof, the statement '$\angle A$ and $\angle B$ are supplementary' means:
options: $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ form a linear pair
questions.explanation: Supplementary angles sum to $180^\circ$. Complementary angles sum to $90^\circ$. While angles that form a linear pair are supplementary, supplementary angles do not have to be adjacent. This definition is fundamental to many geometry proofs.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the core concepts of geometric proofs and congruence: the five congruence criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL), CPCTC, angle relationships with parallel lines, and the Isosceles Triangle Theorem.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review: (1) SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL are the only ways to prove triangles congruent, (2) CPCTC comes AFTER proving triangles congruent, (3) Alternate interior angles are congruent when lines are parallel, (4) AAA proves similarity, not congruence.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) distinguishing between postulates (SSS, SAS, ASA) and consequences (CPCTC), (2) the Isosceles Triangle Theorem: congruent sides $\rightarrow$ congruent opposite angles, (3) HL only works for right triangles, (4) supplementary = $180^\circ$, complementary = $90^\circ$.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Congruence proofs rely on five criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL (right triangles only). CPCTC is used after congruence is proven. Key definitions: midpoint bisects a segment, angle bisector divides an angle, supplementary angles sum to $180^\circ$, complementary sum to $90^\circ$. Parallel lines $\rightarrow$ alternate interior angles congruent, corresponding angles congruent.
